content: For which of the following inputs does binary search take time $O(\log n)$ in the worst case?

options.content: An array of $n$ integers in any order
A linked list of $n$ integers in any order
An array of $n$ integers in increasing order
A linked list of $n$ integers in increasing order

explanation:
Option A: An array of $ n $ integers in any order

  • Binary search works only if the array is sorted.

  • If the array is not sorted, then binary search cannot be used correctly.

  • Even if you try to apply binary search blindly, it won’t correctly find the element.

  • So this option is invalid for binary search’s $ O(\log n) $ time.

❌ Not applicable

Option B: A linked list of $ n $ integers in any order

  • Again, “in any order” means unsorted, so binary search cannot be applied correctly.

  • Even worse, even if the list were sorted, you cannot directly index into the middle of a linked list in $ O(1) $ time—jumping to the middle would take $ O(n) $.

  • So this is even slower, not $ O(\log n) $.

❌ Not applicable

Option C: An array of $ n $ integers in increasing order

  • This is exactly the case where binary search works as intended.

  • On each comparison, you eliminate half the search space.

  • And since random access in an array is $ O(1) $,

    total time in the worst case is $ O(\log n) $.

✅ This is the correct input.

Option D: A linked list of $ n $ integers in increasing order

  • Although it’s sorted, binary search requires random access to the middle element.

  • In a linked list, access to the middle (say position $ n/2 $) takes $ O(n) $ time.

  • So every “midpoint” operation inside the binary search costs $ O(n) $.

  • Total worst‑case time = $ O(n \log n) $, not $ O(\log n) $.

❌ Not $ O(\log n) $

✅ Final Answer:

→ Option C: An array of $ n $ integers in increasing order

content: Suppose that insertion sort is applied to the array $[1,3,5,7,9,11, x, 15,13]$ and it takes exactly two swaps to sort the array. Select all possible values of $x$.
explanation:
We apply insertion sort to:

$ [1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, x, 15, 13] $

and it takes exactly two swaps for the entire sort to finish.

We have to find all possible $ x $.

Step 1. Recall how insertion sort works

Insertion sort processes the array from left to right.

At index $ i $, it takes A[i] (the "key") and inserts it into the correct position among the sorted prefix A[0..i-1], shifting elements larger than the key to the right.

Each swap corresponds to moving the key past one element that is greater than it.

Step 2. The “sorted” version of the array

Fully sorted version of the array will be:

$ [1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 13, 15, x] \quad \text{if } x > 15, $

or

$ [1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11, x, 13, 15] \quad \text{if } 11 < x < 13, $

etc.

But we’ll reason by simulation.

Step 3. Run insertion sort logically, tracking swaps

Up through 11, the array [1,3,5,7,9,11] is sorted.

No swaps so far.

So we start at index 6, value x.

  • Position 6, x:

  • Compare x to 11.

    • If x < 11, we’ll have to shift 11 (and possibly earlier elements).

  • If x > 11, no swaps needed (since the prefix up to 11 is sorted ascending).

Then index 7, 15 (check if it’s in order). Then index 8, 13.

We know insertion sort counts swaps when the current key moves left.

We can proceed case by case for each option.

Case A. $ x = 10 $

Array: [1,3,5,7,9,11,10,15,13]

Step for index 6 (x=10)

  • Compare 10 < 11 → swap once (11 moves right)

  • New array: [1,3,5,7,9,10,11,15,13]

  • 10 > 9, so stop.

→ 1 swap so far.

Step for index 7 (15)

  • 15 > 11 → no swaps.

Step for index 8 (13)

  • Compare 13 < 15 → swap once (15 moves right)

  • Compare 13 > 11 → stop.

  • 1 more swap

Total swaps = 2

✅ Works.

Case B. $ x = 12 $

Array: [1,3,5,7,9,11,12,15,13]

Step for index 6 (x=12)

12 > 11 → no swaps.

Step for index 7 (15)

15 > 12 → no swaps.

Step for index 8 (13)

13 < 15 → one swap (move 15 right)

13 > 12 → stop.

Total = 1 swap

❌ Not enough, we want exactly 2.

Case C. $ x = 14 $

Array: [1,3,5,7,9,11,14,15,13]

Step for x=14

14 > 11 → no swap.

Step for 15

Well placed, no swap.

Step for 13

13 < 15 → swap (1)

Then compare 13 < 14 → swap (2)

Then 13 > 11 → stop.

Total = 2 swaps

✅ Works.

Case D. $ x = 16 $

Array: [1,3,5,7,9,11,16,15,13]

Step for 16

16 > 11 → no swaps.

Step for 15

15 < 16 → one swap (swap 15 & 16)

Now [1,3,5,7,9,11,15,16,13]

Step for 13

13 < 16 → swap (1)

13 < 15 → swap (2)

13 > 11 → stop.

That’s 3 swaps during this step, but insertion sort counts these individually.

Total swaps = 1 (for 15) + 2 (for 13) = 3 swaps.

❌ Too many.

✅ Therefore, the possible values of $ x $ that lead to exactly 2 swaps are:

$ \boxed{x = 10 \text{ or } x = 14.} $

✅ Answer: Option A (10) and Option C (14).
